news_summary: Breakthroughs in medical research are enhancing immune responses to brain cancer, significantly reducing heart risks with a common vaccine, and successfully restoring swallowing function using lab-grown oesophagi.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées de la recherche médicale permettent d'améliorer les réponses immunitaires face au cancer du cerveau, de réduire considérablement les risques cardiaques grâce à un vaccin courant et de rétablir avec succès la fonction de déglutition à l'aide d'œsophages cultivés en laboratoire.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la investigación médica están mejorando la respuesta inmunitaria frente al cáncer de cerebro, reduciendo considerablemente los riesgos cardíacos gracias a una vacuna común y logrando restaurar la función de deglución mediante esófagos cultivados en laboratorio.

articles.title: This virus therapy supercharges the immune system against brain cancer
articles.summary: Scientists have found a way to make one of the most aggressive brain tumors vulnerable to the immune system. A single injection of a modified virus can invade glioblastoma, kill cancer cells, and summon immune fighters deep into the tumor. These immune cells persist and attack, which was linked to longer survival in patients.
articles.content:
Researchers from Mass General Brigham and Dana-Farber Cancer Institute report that a single dose of an oncolytic virus, a genetically engineered virus designed to infect and kill cancer cells, can draw immune cells deep into brain tumors and keep them active there. Their findings, published in Cell, explain how this approach improved survival in patients with glioblastoma, the most aggressive and common form of primary brain cancer, during a recent clinical trial.

"Patients with glioblastoma have not benefited from immunotherapies that have transformed patient care in other cancer types such as melanoma because glioblastoma is a 'cold' tumor with poor infiltration by cancer-fighting immune cells," said co-senior author Kai Wucherpfennig, MD, PhD, chair of the Department of Cancer Immunology and Virology at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute. "Findings from our clinical trial and our mechanistic study show that is now feasible to bring these critical immune cells into glioblastoma."

Engineered Herpes Virus Targets Cancer Cells

The therapy uses an oncolytic virus developed by E. Antonio Chiocca, MD, PhD, Executive Director of the Center for Tumors of the Nervous System at Mass General Brigham Cancer Institute. It is based on a modified herpes simplex virus that has been engineered to replicate only inside glioblastoma cells, leaving healthy tissue unaffected.

Once inside a tumor cell, the virus destroys it and then produces copies of itself that move on to infect neighboring cancer cells. This process not only kills tumor cells directly but also activates the immune system. In a phase 1 clinical trial involving 41 patients with recurrent glioblastoma, treatment with the virus was associated with longer survival compared to historical outcomes, with the strongest benefit seen in patients who already had antibodies against the virus.

Immune Response Linked to Longer Survival

To better understand how the therapy works, the researchers analyzed tumor samples from trial participants. They found that the treatment led to a sustained presence of immune T cells within the tumors. Patients whose cytotoxic T cells were located closer to dying tumor cells tended to survive longer after treatment.

The therapy also boosted the number of existing T cells in the brain, suggesting that it strengthens the body's own immune defenses rather than relying solely on new immune activity.

"We show that increased infiltration of T cells that are attacking tumor cells translates into a therapeutic benefit for patients with glioblastoma," said Chiocca, who is also a co-senior author of the study. "Our findings could have important implications for a cancer whose standard of care hasn't changed for 20 years."

In addition to Wucherpfennig and Chiocca, authors include Maxime Meylan, Ye Tian, Lijian Wu, Alexander L. Ling, Daniel Kovarsky, Graham L. Barlow, Linh D. Nguyen, Jason Pyrdol, Sascha Marx, Lucas Westphal, Julius Michel, L. Nicolas Gonzalez Castro, Sydney Dumont, Andres Santos, Itay Tirosh, and Mario L. Suva.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where a genetically engineered virus therapy effectively targets and kills glioblastoma brain cancer cells while activating the immune system, leading to longer patient survival. This represents a meaningful advancement in treatment for a highly aggressive cancer with previously limited options, impacting a broad patient population. The article provides detailed context about the therapy, clinical trial results, and the underlying mechanism, fulfilling criteria for substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: New virus treatment helps immune system fight aggressive brain cancer
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Glioblastoma is one of the most aggressive and common brain cancers. It is hard to treat because it does not respond well to current immune therapies. This type of tumor usually keeps immune cells away, making it a “cold” tumor.

**What happened** – Scientists tested a new therapy using a genetically changed virus based on herpes. This virus only infects and kills glioblastoma cells. After a single injection, the virus destroys cancer cells and helps bring immune cells called T cells deep inside the tumor. In a clinical trial with 41 patients, those treated with the virus lived longer than expected.

**Impact** – This therapy is important because it shows that immune cells can be brought into glioblastoma tumors where they can attack cancer cells. Patients with more immune cells close to dying tumor cells survived longer. This is a big step because glioblastoma treatments have not improved much in 20 years.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will likely continue testing this virus therapy in more patients to confirm its benefits. They may also explore combining it with other treatments to make it even stronger against brain cancer.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A modified virus treatment can boost the immune system’s attack on glioblastoma brain tumors, helping patients live longer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau traitement viral aide le système immunitaire à combattre un cancer du cerveau agressif
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le glioblastome est l’un des cancers du cerveau les plus agressifs et fréquents. Il est difficile à traiter car il ne répond pas bien aux immunothérapies actuelles. Ce type de tumeur empêche généralement les cellules immunitaires d’entrer, ce qui la rend « froide ».

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont testé une nouvelle thérapie utilisant un virus génétiquement modifié basé sur l’herpès. Ce virus infecte et tue uniquement les cellules de glioblastome. Après une seule injection, le virus détruit les cellules cancéreuses et aide à faire entrer des cellules immunitaires appelées lymphocytes T profondément dans la tumeur. Dans un essai clinique avec 41 patients, ceux traités avec ce virus ont vécu plus longtemps que prévu.

**Impact** – Cette thérapie est importante car elle montre que les cellules immunitaires peuvent être amenées dans les tumeurs de glioblastome où elles peuvent attaquer les cellules cancéreuses. Les patients ayant plus de cellules immunitaires proches des cellules tumorales mourantes ont survécu plus longtemps. C’est un grand progrès car les traitements contre le glioblastome n’ont pas beaucoup évolué depuis 20 ans.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement continuer à tester cette thérapie virale sur plus de patients pour confirmer ses bienfaits. Ils pourraient aussi explorer des combinaisons avec d’autres traitements pour la rendre encore plus efficace contre le cancer du cerveau.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un traitement par virus modifié peut renforcer l’attaque du système immunitaire contre les tumeurs cérébrales de glioblastome, aidant les patients à vivre plus longtemps.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo tratamiento viral ayuda al sistema inmunológico a combatir un cáncer cerebral agresivo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El glioblastoma es uno de los cánceres cerebrales más agresivos y comunes. Es difícil de tratar porque no responde bien a las inmunoterapias actuales. Este tipo de tumor generalmente mantiene alejadas a las células inmunitarias, por lo que se llama tumor “frío”.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos probaron una nueva terapia usando un virus modificado genéticamente basado en el herpes. Este virus solo infecta y mata las células de glioblastoma. Después de una sola inyección, el virus destruye las células cancerosas y ayuda a que las células inmunitarias llamadas linfocitos T entren profundamente en el tumor. En un ensayo clínico con 41 pacientes, quienes recibieron el virus vivieron más tiempo de lo esperado.

**Impacto** – Esta terapia es importante porque muestra que las células inmunitarias pueden entrar en los tumores de glioblastoma donde pueden atacar las células cancerosas. Los pacientes con más células inmunitarias cerca de células tumorales moribundas vivieron más tiempo. Esto es un avance porque los tratamientos para glioblastoma no han mejorado mucho en 20 años.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente continuarán probando esta terapia viral en más pacientes para confirmar sus beneficios. También podrían combinarla con otros tratamientos para hacerla aún más fuerte contra el cáncer cerebral.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un tratamiento con virus modificado puede aumentar el ataque del sistema inmunológico contra tumores cerebrales de glioblastoma, ayudando a que los pacientes vivan más tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, stylized brain tumor depicted as a dark cluster being gently surrounded and penetrated by vibrant, radiant virus-shaped orbs that emit light, with numerous bright, active T-cell-like figures—shown as small, luminous, shield-bearing silhouettes—marching into the tumor cluster, symbolizing the immune system’s energized attack and sustained presence within the brain cancer.

articles.title: This common vaccine cuts heart risk nearly in half in new study
articles.summary: A shingles vaccine might double as a powerful heart protector. In people already at high risk, it cut major cardiac events by 46% and deaths by an impressive 66% within a year. Scientists think preventing shingles may also stop clot-related complications that can lead to heart attacks and strokes. The effect is so strong, it rivals the benefits of quitting smoking.
articles.content:
People living with heart disease who received a shingles vaccine experienced nearly half the rate of serious heart-related events within a year compared with those who were not vaccinated. These findings come from research being presented at the American College of Cardiology's Annual Scientific Session (ACC.26).

The study reviewed data from more than 246,822 adults in the United States diagnosed with atherosclerotic heart disease, which is caused by plaque buildup in the arteries. The results add to growing evidence that the shingles vaccine may offer benefits beyond preventing shingles, including a reduced risk of conditions like heart disease and even dementia.

"This vaccine has been found over and over again to have cardioprotective effects for reducing heart attack, stroke and death," said Robert Nguyen, MD, a resident physician at the University of California, Riverside and the study's lead author. "Looking at the highest risk population, those with existing cardiovascular disease, these protective effects might be even greater than among the general public."

Why the Shingles Vaccine May Protect the Heart

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) recommends the shingles vaccine for adults age 50 years and older, as well as younger individuals with weakened immune systems. The vaccine protects against herpes zoster, a condition that causes a painful rash and can sometimes lead to long-lasting nerve pain. It develops when the virus responsible for chickenpox becomes active again in the body, often years after the original infection.

Earlier research has shown that shingles infections can trigger blood clot formation near the brain and heart, increasing the risk of heart attacks, strokes and venous thromboembolism. By preventing shingles, the vaccine may also help reduce the likelihood of these dangerous clotting events.

Large Study Shows Significant Risk Reduction

To conduct the study, researchers used TriNetX, a large database containing medical records from millions of Americans. They examined adults age 50 years or older with atherosclerotic disease between 2018-2025. The analysis included 123,411 people who had received at least one dose of the Shingrix or Zostavax vaccine and an equal number who had not been vaccinated. Both groups were similar in terms of demographics and other health conditions.

Researchers focused on heart-related outcomes occurring between one month and one year after vaccination (or the same timeframe for those who were not vaccinated). Across all measures, vaccinated individuals showed lower risk. They were 46% less likely to experience a major adverse cardiac event and 66% less likely to die from any cause. In addition, their risk of heart attack dropped by 32%, stroke by 25%, and heart failure by 25%.

Nguyen noted that these reductions are substantial and comparable to the benefits seen from quitting smoking. He added that the findings reinforce current recommendations for adults over age 50 to receive the vaccine.

"Vaccines are one of the most important medicines we have to prevent disease," he said. "Sometimes patients are unsure about whether they should get a vaccine or not, particularly in an age of disinformation. These results provide another reason for them to elect to get the vaccine."

Limitations and Long-Term Impact

The analysis only tracked outcomes during the first year after vaccination, so the long-term effects may differ. However, a previous study published in 2025 found that shingles vaccination was linked to a 23% reduction in cardiovascular events in generally healthy adults, with benefits that could last up to eight years.

One limitation is that people who choose to get vaccinated may also engage in healthier behaviors overall. While researchers adjusted for several health and socioeconomic factors (problems related to housing and economic circumstances, social environment, employment status, education and literacy), it is still possible that some of the observed benefit is influenced by these differences. Even so, the study's large sample size and statistical approach provide strong evidence that shingles vaccination is associated with a meaningful reduction in heart risk.

Nguyen will present the study, "Herpes Zoster Vaccination and Risk of Cardiovascular Events in Patients with Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease," on Monday, March 30, at 12:30 p.m. CT / 17:30 UTC in Posters, Hall E.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical study showing that the shingles vaccine substantially reduces major cardiac events and deaths in people with heart disease, offering a powerful, measurable health benefit with broad public impact. The study is well-detailed, focuses on a single breakthrough, and highlights a vaccine's potential beyond its original purpose, which is inspiring and hopeful for public health.
articles.personality_title: Shingles vaccine lowers heart risks by nearly half in people with heart disease
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Shingles is a painful rash caused by the reactivation of the chickenpox virus. It can lead to serious nerve pain and is common in adults over 50. People with heart disease are at risk of heart attacks and strokes, sometimes linked to blood clots triggered by shingles infections.

**What happened** – A new study looked at over 246,000 adults in the U.S. with heart disease. Researchers compared those who got the shingles vaccine (Shingrix or Zostavax) to those who did not. Within one year after vaccination, vaccinated people had 46% fewer serious heart problems and 66% fewer deaths. They also had fewer heart attacks, strokes, and heart failure events.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows the shingles vaccine does more than just prevent shingles. It also helps protect the heart by reducing the risk of dangerous blood clots. The benefit is so strong that it is similar to the health gains from quitting smoking. This offers a new reason for people with heart disease to get vaccinated.

**What's next step** – The study will be presented at a major heart health conference soon. Researchers hope this will encourage more adults over 50, especially those with heart disease, to get the shingles vaccine. Future studies may look at how long these heart benefits last and if the vaccine helps other groups.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Getting the shingles vaccine can nearly cut in half the chance of serious heart problems and death in people with heart disease within a year.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le vaccin contre le zona réduit de moitié les risques cardiaques chez les malades du cœur
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le zona est une éruption douloureuse causée par la réactivation du virus de la varicelle. Il peut entraîner des douleurs nerveuses graves et touche souvent les adultes de plus de 50 ans. Les personnes atteintes de maladies cardiaques risquent des crises cardiaques et des AVC, parfois liés à des caillots sanguins déclenchés par le zona.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle étude a analysé plus de 246 000 adultes aux États-Unis souffrant de maladies cardiaques. Les chercheurs ont comparé ceux ayant reçu le vaccin contre le zona (Shingrix ou Zostavax) à ceux non vaccinés. Un an après la vaccination, les vaccinés avaient 46 % d'événements cardiaques graves en moins et 66 % de décès en moins. Ils ont aussi eu moins d'infarctus, d'AVC et d'insuffisance cardiaque.

**Impact** – Cela montre que le vaccin contre le zona fait plus que prévenir le zona. Il protège aussi le cœur en réduisant le risque de caillots dangereux. Cet avantage est comparable à celui d'arrêter de fumer. C’est une nouvelle raison importante pour les malades du cœur de se faire vacciner.

**Prochaine étape** – L’étude sera présentée lors d’une grande conférence sur la santé cardiaque. Les chercheurs espèrent que cela encouragera plus d’adultes de plus de 50 ans, surtout ceux ayant une maladie cardiaque, à se faire vacciner. D’autres recherches pourraient étudier la durée de ces bénéfices cardiaques et l’effet chez d’autres groupes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Se faire vacciner contre le zona peut réduire de moitié les risques de graves problèmes cardiaques et de décès chez les malades du cœur en un an.
articles.personality_title_es: La vacuna contra el herpes zóster reduce casi a la mitad el riesgo cardíaco en personas con enfermedades del corazón
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El herpes zóster es una erupción dolorosa causada por la reactivación del virus de la varicela. Puede causar dolor nervioso grave y es común en adultos mayores de 50 años. Las personas con enfermedades del corazón tienen riesgo de ataques cardíacos y accidentes cerebrovasculares, a veces relacionados con coágulos sanguíneos provocados por el herpes zóster.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio analizó a más de 246,000 adultos en Estados Unidos con enfermedad cardíaca. Los investigadores compararon a quienes recibieron la vacuna contra el herpes zóster (Shingrix o Zostavax) con quienes no la recibieron. Al año de la vacunación, los vacunados tuvieron un 46 % menos de eventos cardíacos graves y un 66 % menos de muertes. También presentaron menos ataques cardíacos, accidentes cerebrovasculares y fallas cardíacas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que la vacuna contra el herpes zóster hace más que prevenir esta enfermedad. También protege el corazón al reducir el riesgo de coágulos peligrosos. El beneficio es tan fuerte que es parecido al de dejar de fumar. Esto ofrece una nueva razón para que las personas con enfermedades del corazón se vacunen.

**Próximo paso** – El estudio será presentado en una importante conferencia sobre salud cardíaca pronto. Los investigadores esperan que esto anime a más adultos mayores de 50 años, especialmente con enfermedades cardíacas, a vacunarse contra el herpes zóster. Futuras investigaciones podrían estudiar cuánto duran estos beneficios para el corazón y si la vacuna ayuda a otros grupos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Recibir la vacuna contra el herpes zóster puede reducir casi a la mitad la probabilidad de sufrir problemas cardíacos graves y morir en personas con enfermedades del corazón en un año.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, healthy heart cradled gently within a protective shield shaped like a vaccine vial, surrounded by soft, glowing artery-like branches free of plaque, set against a calm, natural background of muted earth tones.

articles.title: Lab-grown oesophagus restores pigs’ ability to swallow
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 20 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00936-8Engineered tissue could eventually be used for children born with gaps in their alimentary canal, or for adults whose muscles have been damaged by cancer.
articles.content:
Pigs that received bioengineered oesophagi were able to swallow food normally.Credit: Fotosmurf03/iStock via Getty

Scientists have used stem cells to make bioengineered oesophagi that they successfully implanted into pigs, restoring the animals’ ability to swallow and eat. Similar lab-grown structures could be used to treat people with cancer or other conditions affecting the muscular tube that connects the throat to the stomach, researchers say.

Paolo De Coppi, a paediatric surgeon and researcher at University College London, says his team has been investigating minimally invasive ways to treat children born with a large hole in their oesophagus, a condition called long-gap oesophageal atresia. The current treatment is to move the child’s stomach up to their neck and join it directly to the back of their throat, or to transplant part of their colon to bridge the gap.

De Coppi and his colleagues have previously grown grown mouse cells on a rat oesophagus and implanted them into mice, and have transplanted the pig-based scaffold into rabbits. The team’s latest work, published in Nature Biotechnology today1, involved transplanting sections of oesophagus that had been grown in the lab into pigs, which are better than rodents as a model for humans, because of their size and physiology.

The ability to generate an oesophagus with the necessary components that also functions normally is impressive, says Andrew Barbour, an academic surgeon at the Frazer Institute of the University of Queensland in Brisbane, Australia. The grafts developed some scar tissue — which causes issues with swallowing — but this reduced over time, which is also promising, he adds.

Transplant option

To grow oesophagi in the lab, De Coppi and his colleagues started with small samples of muscle cells and connective tissue from the recipient pigs and used them to make two kinds of stem cell, which can be turned into other types of cell. They also took the oesophagi from 16 other young pigs and removed the original cells to create oesophagus scaffolds. The team injected each scaffold with the recipient pig’s stem cells. Over two months, the cells grew across the scaffold to create a graft. The researchers used 10-kilogram minipigs, in part to approximate the size of the children who could one day be treated with the method.

The surgeons then removed 2.5-centimetre segments of oesophagus from the eight recipient pigs, and replaced them with segments of the oesophagus scaffolds. These were covered with a biodegradable mesh tube to help blood vessels develop.

Five of the pigs survived for the whole six-month study period; they showed functioning muscle, nerves and blood vessels, the team notes, and were able to swallow. The remaining three pigs were killed early for humane reasons.

From pigs to people
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where bioengineered oesophagi grown from stem cells were successfully implanted in pigs, restoring their ability to swallow. This advancement has broad implications for treating children born with oesophageal defects and adults with muscle damage from cancer, representing a meaningful, tangible improvement in medical treatment options with potential large-scale impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists restore pigs’ swallowing by implanting lab-grown oesophagus
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some children are born with a serious problem where their oesophagus, the tube that connects the throat to the stomach, has a big gap. Adults can also lose oesophagus function due to cancer. Current treatments are difficult and can cause complications.

**What happened** – Researchers at University College London used stem cells to grow new oesophagus tissue in the lab. They created scaffolds from pig oesophagus and added the pigs’ own stem cells to grow new tissue. Then, they replaced damaged sections in eight pigs with these lab-grown oesophagi. Five pigs survived the six-month study and were able to swallow normally.

**Impact** – This is the first time bioengineered oesophagi have worked well in large animals like pigs, whose size and body functions are similar to humans. The new oesophagi had working muscles, nerves, and blood vessels. Some scar tissue formed but it improved over time, showing the grafts can heal and function properly.

**What's next step** – The team aims to improve the method and reduce scarring. They hope to eventually test this treatment in children with oesophageal defects and adults needing oesophagus repair. The success in pigs is an important step toward human trials.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have grown and implanted lab-made oesophagi in pigs, restoring their ability to swallow and paving the way for new treatments in humans.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques restaurent la capacité d’avaler chez des cochons grâce à un œsophage cultivé en laboratoire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certains enfants naissent avec un problème grave où leur œsophage, le tube reliant la gorge à l’estomac, présente un grand trou. Les adultes peuvent aussi perdre la fonction de leur œsophage à cause du cancer. Les traitements actuels sont difficiles et peuvent entraîner des complications.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du University College London ont utilisé des cellules souches pour cultiver un nouvel œsophage en laboratoire. Ils ont créé des supports à partir d’œsophages de cochons et y ont ajouté les propres cellules souches des cochons pour faire pousser de nouveaux tissus. Ensuite, ils ont remplacé des sections endommagées chez huit cochons par ces œsophages cultivés. Cinq cochons ont survécu six mois et ont pu avaler normalement.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des œsophages bio-ingénierés fonctionnent bien chez de grands animaux comme les cochons, dont la taille et les fonctions sont proches de celles des humains. Les nouveaux œsophages avaient des muscles, des nerfs et des vaisseaux sanguins fonctionnels. Un peu de tissu cicatriciel s’est formé, mais il s’est amélioré avec le temps, montrant que les greffes peuvent guérir et fonctionner correctement.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe veut améliorer la méthode et réduire les cicatrices. Ils espèrent tester ce traitement chez des enfants avec des défauts œsophagiens et des adultes ayant besoin de réparations. Le succès chez les cochons est une étape importante avant les essais humains.

**Une phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont cultivé et implanté des œsophages en laboratoire chez des cochons, restaurant leur capacité à avaler et ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements pour les humains.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos restauran la capacidad de tragar en cerdos con esófago cultivado en laboratorio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunos niños nacen con un problema grave donde su esófago, el tubo que conecta la garganta con el estómago, tiene un gran agujero. Los adultos también pueden perder la función del esófago debido al cáncer. Los tratamientos actuales son difíciles y pueden causar complicaciones.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del University College London usaron células madre para cultivar un nuevo esófago en el laboratorio. Crearon estructuras a partir del esófago de cerdos y añadieron las propias células madre de los cerdos para hacer crecer nuevo tejido. Luego reemplazaron secciones dañadas en ocho cerdos con estos esófagos cultivados. Cinco cerdos sobrevivieron seis meses y pudieron tragar normalmente.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que esófagos bioingenierizados funcionan bien en animales grandes como los cerdos, cuyo tamaño y funciones corporales son similares a las humanas. Los nuevos esófagos tenían músculos, nervios y vasos sanguíneos que funcionaban. Se formó algo de tejido cicatricial, pero mejoró con el tiempo, mostrando que los injertos pueden sanar y funcionar bien.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo quiere mejorar el método y reducir las cicatrices. Esperan probar este tratamiento en niños con defectos esofágicos y adultos que necesitan reparación. El éxito en cerdos es un paso importante hacia ensayos en humanos.

**Una frase clave** – Científicos han cultivado e implantado esófagos en laboratorio en cerdos, restaurando su capacidad para tragar y abriendo camino a nuevos tratamientos para humanos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gentle pig calmly eating from a rustic wooden bowl, with a translucent, glowing, anatomically shaped oesophagus scaffold subtly overlaid inside its neck and chest area, surrounded by soft, natural earth tones and delicate hints of cellular patterns blending into the background.
